title: Marketing Consulting Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541613 ($34,007,872,680) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541613 ($79,535,987,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
sources: BLS QCEW 2024 annual averages (US000, private)
BLS ECEC news release (raw/ecec.nr0.htm)
Census SUSB 2022 (receipts by NAICS)

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $282,178 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541613); class <5: 41,556 firms, avg revenue/firm $395,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,884 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,444 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,727,383 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $270,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,925 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,595,461 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $562,690 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,662 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,903,738 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,393,435 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 274 firms, avg revenue/firm $47,364,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,412,501 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $120,739,011 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,895,655 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,936
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

current_adoption_pct.note: Share of US marketing firms with AI materially embedded in core delivery workflows. CMO Survey Fall 2024 (Duke/Deloitte/AMA): GenAI used in 11.1% of all marketing activities; B2B Services: 16.7%. Gartner 2026 CMO Spend Survey (n=401, Jan-Mar 2026): only 30% of CMOs report mature or fully developed AI readiness to scale capabilities. Supermetrics 2026 Marketing Data Report (n=435, Oct-Dec 2025): only 6% have fully embedded AI into workflows. Salesforce State of Marketing 10th Edition (survey Oct-Nov 2025): 75% use AI in at least one workflow but only 39% fully integrated. The 30% Gartner figure best represents materially using AI in core delivery — distinguishing systematic embedding from ad-hoc or single-tool usage. Reconciliation: 6% (Supermetrics full embed) to 30% (Gartner ready to scale) bracket; use 0.30 as upper-bound of systematic embedding including firms with material but incomplete integration.
sources: Gartner 2026 CMO Spend Survey (May 2026)
CMO Survey Fall 2024 (Duke Fuqua/Deloitte/AMA, Nov 2024)
Supermetrics 2026 Marketing Data Report (published 2026, data Oct-Dec 2025)
Salesforce State of Marketing 10th Edition (survey Oct-Nov 2025)

historical_analogs.benchmark: Marketing automation adoption: <10% of companies in 2015 (Forbes, Aug 2015); early-mover platforms (Eloqua, Marketo, Pardot) commercialized ~2006-2010; Serious Decisions projected 50% adoption by 2015 from 2012 baseline (~10-15% then); actual broad-market 50% crossed approximately 2020-2021 based on trajectory to 76% by 2024-2026. Elapsed time from ~10% (2012) to 50% (~2020): approximately 8 years for broad market. AI in marketing consulting started meaningful adoption ~2023-2024. Applying 8-year analog from 10% start: 50% adoption by ~2031-2032. However AI diffusion is faster; adjusting to 5-6 years given steeper S-curve and client-side AI compression.
sources: Forbes Aug 2015 (Mike Templeman, less than 10% of companies use marketing automation)
Emailmonday Ultimate Marketing Automation Statistics 2026
Backlinko Marketing Automation Statistics 2026
CMO Survey Fall 2024

owners_60plus_pct.note: 50% of small employer businesses (5-500 employees) owned by people aged 60+. Source: Exit Planning Institute data cited in McKinsey Institute for Economic Mobility 2024 report; corroborated by EPI National Exit Planners Survey showing average age of US business owner is 67 by end of 2024. Not marketing-consulting specific — ESTIMATE flag applied. General small business / professional services demographic applies across service sectors. ABS (replacing discontinued SBO post-2012) does not publish sector-level owner age breakdowns at 6-digit NAICS.
sources: Exit Planning Institute / McKinsey Institute for Economic Mobility 2024
EPI Fifth Annual National Exit Planners Survey 2024
Census ABS (replacement for SBO, no marketing-consulting breakdown available)
owners_60plus_pct.succession_note: EPI data: only 32% of owners have a documented exit plan; most exits end in closure rather than transfer due to lack of succession pathways. Succession shortage documented = true.

active_consolidators.note: PE-backed platforms doing add-on acquisitions specifically in marketing consulting/advisory (541613, distinct from 541810 advertising agencies or digital media buying) in the last 24 months. Named platforms with relevant activity: (1) Stagwell Inc. (public, PE-origin): add-ons in marketing strategy and advisory niches (TrueLogic, Kettle Solutions, Ramenu), primarily 2023-2024; (2) Hero Digital (PE-backed by Centre Lane Partners): acquired Huge (Oct 2024), positions as marketing strategy and experience consulting; (3) Accordion Partners: PE-focused management consulting including marketing/commercial advisory to PE-backed portcos. General observations: PE activity in marketing services is robust but concentrated in performance marketing, martech, ecommerce, and influencer, not pure strategy consulting. Houlihan Lokey Q2 2025: 266 marketing services transactions in 1H 2025 but dominated by digital/media. Pure 541613 consulting rollups are sparse; count of 3 is conservative-to-middle estimate.
sources: Evalla Advisors Q1 2025 M&A Landscape in Marketing newsletter
Tracxn Stagwell Global acquisitions list Apr 2026
Privsource Consulting Acquisitions 2024
Houlihan Lokey Q2 2025 marketing services sector update via mergersandacquisitions.net

buy_mult.note: EV/EBITDA for small marketing consulting firm acquisitions ($1-5M EBITDA), after normalizing for market-rate owner/partner compensation. CT Acquisitions (citing Pitchbook/GF Data/Equiteq/Capstone, 2024-2026): pure project-based strategy boutiques with $1-5M EBITDA: 4-5x EBITDA; midpoint = 4.5x. First Page Sage (Q1 2025, marketing consulting $1-3M EBITDA): 10.1x — judged elevated as it likely includes martech/performance agencies with recurring SaaS-like revenue, not representative of pure 541613 strategy boutiques. BizBuySell 2024 broad SDE average 2.57x; professional services consulting SDE range 1.29-3.30x; SDE-to-EBITDA conversion at ~$275K owner add-back on $1M SDE yields ~$1.275M EBITDA, giving EV/EBITDA of ~2.0-2.3x — too low for marketed, professionally advised transactions. CT Acquisitions figure (4-5x) is most directly applicable: sourced from PE-quality data for lower-middle-market consulting with owner comp already normalized out. Use 4.5 (midpoint).
sources: CT Acquisitions Consulting Business Valuation Guide 2026 (citing Pitchbook, GF Data, Equiteq)
First Page Sage Marketing Agency EBITDA Multiples Q1 2025
BizBuySell Insight Report 2024
ClearlyAcquired Valuation Multiples for Consulting and Professional Service Firms

exit_mult.note: EV/EBITDA for platform-scale marketing consulting ($10M+ EBITDA). Equiteq ESG Consulting M&A Report 2025: private market EV/EBITDA avg 11.9x (premium specialty segment). Equiteq Life Sciences Consulting Aug 2025: median 15.3x (also premium). Mid-market consulting M&A 2025: PE buyers paid avg 10.1x across all consulting (clfi.co.uk 2025). CT Acquisitions: established mid-market consulting 5-7x EBITDA. For platform-scale marketing consulting (not premium ESG/life-sciences; not martech SaaS): 7-9x range reflects platform premium over boutique while discounting below specialty consulting. First Page Sage marketing consulting $5-10M EBITDA = 13.5x, judged elevated. Use 8.0x as midpoint of 7-9x range for platform-scale marketing consulting with multi-year retainer mix and some institutional client base.
sources: Equiteq ESG Consulting M&A Report 2025
Equiteq Life Sciences Consulting Aug 2025
CT Acquisitions Consulting Business Valuation Guide 2026
clfi.co.uk M&A EV/EBITDA Multiples 2025 PE vs Corporate by Sector
First Page Sage Consulting Firm EBITDA Multiples Q1 2025

pricing_structure.summary: Boutique marketing consultants primarily bill via monthly retainers (~50-60% of revenue) for ongoing strategic advisory and continuous execution; project/fixed fees (~25-30%) for defined deliverables; performance-based/revenue-share (~5-10%); hourly/day-rate (~5-10%). Influencer Marketing Hub 2026 survey: 78% of digital agencies use retainer-based pricing as primary model (up from 64% in 2023). Day rates typically $500-$800/day for US boutique marketing consultants; retainers typically $5,000-$15,000/month for ongoing positioning and strategy work.
sources: Influencer Marketing Hub 2026 Digital Agency Pricing Survey
NetSuite Marketing Agency Retainers overview 2025
Breef Marketing Agency Pricing Models 2025
GTM 8020 Marketing Consultant Cost guide 2025

13-1111_management_analysts.rationale: Strategy analysts, market researchers, competitive analysts. Heavy AI exposure in research synthesis, positioning decks, persona work. Mid-range 0.40-0.55.

11-1021_general_ops_managers.rationale: Owner-operators and principals. Client relationship, selling, judgment. Slightly higher than pure mgmt consulting because internal marketing coordination automates more. Mid-range 0.10-0.15.

13-1161_market_research_analysts.rationale: Directly in AI crosshairs — competitive intel, market sizing, survey analysis. Mid-range 0.55-0.70.

41-3091_sales_reps.rationale: Proposal/pitch deck production automates; relationship selling persists. Mid-range 0.30-0.40.

13-1082_pm_specialists.rationale: Status reporting, scheduling automate substantially. Mid-range 0.40-0.50.

tail.rationale: For 541613, tail contains content creators, designers, digital strategists, social media specialists — among most AI-exposed workers. Creative content production, ad copy, social posts, email drafts, blog content being automated at pace. Mid-range 0.55-0.65.

pi_dist.rationale: Mid-range of 0.25-0.35. Highly effort-anchored (day-rate retainers renegotiated at renewal). Key-person risk extreme (85% of firms <5 employees; client book walks with founder). No license, no proprietary data moat in most cases. Specialized vertical expertise and CMO trust provide some floor but do not sustain pricing power as AI compresses effort. Declared at 0.30.

pi_moat.rationale: Mid-range of 0.18-0.28. Very thin moat: no license, no statutory gate, no proprietary data clients cannot access elsewhere. Client-side AI directly competes with research, strategy, and content work. Only vertical specialization, martech execution expertise, or integrated data/media work creates switching costs. Declared at 0.23.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.30 (30% with material AI embedding in core workflows, as of early 2026). Historical analog: marketing automation went from ~10% (2012) to ~50% broad market by ~2020, approximately 8 years. However: (1) AI S-curve is steeper — current_adoption_pct already at 30% by year 2-3 of meaningful adoption (2023-2026); (2) client-side AI compresses the gap; (3) Gartner projects marketing AI automation of work to double from 16% to 36% by 2028. Adjusting 8-year historical analog downward: if 30% reached by 2026 (year ~3 from meaningful start 2023), 50% on accelerated curve by ~2028-2029 (year 5-6 from today). Declare t50 = 5 years from 2026, implying 50% by 2031.

terminal_value.justification: No statutory license or proprietary data moat protects marketing advisory services. Client-side AI (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude) directly substitutes for competitive research, strategy decks, persona work, and content production — historically the highest-billing activities of marketing consultants. Demand shifts toward CMO relationship management, martech implementation oversight, and integrated analytics interpretation, which are harder to automate, but the billable scope shrinks materially. Classification PRESSURED (not MELTING) because: (1) CMO trust and senior relationships are genuine switching cost; (2) complex multi-channel execution and martech orchestration require human accountability; (3) many boutiques will consolidate into platform firms with deeper data assets. Classification not DURABLE because: thin moat (pi_moat = 0.23), effort-anchored billing erodes capture rate as AI compresses effort, no license gates, and client AI tools are free substitutes for core deliverables.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: CONFIRMED. V computed solely from declared ai_replaceable_share applied to role-mix wage_shares and labor_share — no deal activity evidence used. A computed solely from t50_years derived from technology adoption analog (marketing automation historical timeline) and current_adoption_pct from surveys — no deal activity evidence used. active_consolidators (3) informed only B (via CFD) and M (buy_mult/exit_mult are independent research inputs). No cross-contamination of deal flow data into V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Revenue mix: ~50-60% monthly retainers (effort-anchored, renegotiated at renewal), ~25-30% project/fixed fees, ~10-20% performance or other. Retainer renewal pricing tracks day-rate benchmarks, not client-perceived value delivered. When AI compresses a 10-day engagement to 3 days, the consultant retains margin only if they can hold billing rates at renewal or shift to value-based pricing. Client visibility into effort is high in boutique relationships, enabling renegotiation. Structural deflation risk is severe: retainers drive majority of revenue and are the most effort-exposed. Estimate: 60-70% of revenue base is directly subject to effort-anchored pricing deflation as AI adoption accelerates. C = 0.69 reflects this — pi_dist = 0.30 captures the renegotiation risk at renewal, pi_moat = 0.23 captures the absence of barriers preventing client defection or in-house AI substitution.
cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 1.409pp (V_raw 0.2206 / margin 0.1565). A = 2.0 (t50=5). Uplift < 3pp while A=2.0 (not ≥5), so no inconsistency trigger applies. However, low uplift reflects moderate labor_share * moderate ars; even with full adoption, EBITDA margin expansion is limited by the low pi_dist and pi_moat (C=0.69). No revision needed.

top_uncertain_inputs.reason: The tail (wage_share = 0.4727) is the single largest component of the ai_replaceable_share computation, contributing 0.2836 of 0.4838. It is entirely declared judgment (fraction = 0.60) with no OEWS occupation-level breakdown. A 0.05 change in tail fraction changes ai_replaceable_share by ~0.024 and V_raw by ~0.011, shifting V by ~0.44 points.
top_uncertain_inputs.sensitivity: tail fraction 0.55 → V = 8.59; tail fraction 0.65 → V = 9.04

top_uncertain_inputs.reason: Sources diverge substantially: CT Acquisitions/Pitchbook 4-5x vs First Page Sage 10.1x for same size range. Choice of buy_mult determines M score. At buy_mult = 6.0 (compromise), M = clamp(4×(8-6)/6, 0, 10) = clamp(1.333, 0, 10) = 1.333, S drops to ~2.7. At buy_mult = 4.0, M = clamp(4×4/4, 0, 10) = 4.0, S rises to ~3.3.

Analogical derivation with high uncertainty. If AI adoption in marketing consulting is slower (t50 = 8 years, matching full marketing automation analog): A = 10/8 = 1.25, S drops to ~2.9. If faster (t50 = 3 years): A = 10/3 = 3.33, S rises to ~3.6.
top_uncertain_inputs.sensitivity: t50 range 3-8 years → A range 1.25-3.33 → S range ~2.9-3.6

top_uncertain_inputs.reason: C = 10 × pi_dist × pi_moat = 0.69. Both declared at midpoints of their ranges. At extremes: pi_dist=0.25, pi_moat=0.18 → C=0.45; pi_dist=0.35, pi_moat=0.28 → C=0.98. C range 0.45-0.98 → S range ~2.9-3.4.

reviewer_note: 541613 Marketing Consulting presents the highest V score achievable under this model framework (V=8.82) given the combination of high labor_share (0.4559) and the unusually AI-exposed role and tail mix: content creation, research synthesis, competitive intelligence, strategy decks, and persona work are exactly the tasks generative AI addresses first. The terminal value classification PRESSURED rather than MELTING reflects genuine survivorship via CMO trust relationships and martech orchestration, but reviewers should note that the thin moat (C=0.69) means this industry's protective factors are among the weakest in any professional services segment. The B score (8.31) is high relative to S (3.16), indicating demographic-driven exit pressure and consolidation opportunity are real, but the spread M (3.11) is moderate — the multiple arbitrage between boutique (4.5x) and platform (8.0x) exists but is compressed relative to higher-moat professional services where platform premiums reach 12-15x. The key risk to the score is the buy_mult input: if true market-clearing prices for $1-5M EBITDA marketing consulting boutiques are closer to 6-7x (reflecting frothy 2024-2025 market conditions for any recurring-revenue services), M collapses and S falls to ~2.7-2.9, changing the risk tier materially.
